Today I'm really excited to go over with you a piece for again boardroom bottom line and it's for a book of a called Encyclopedia of Estate Planning. So I think this piece is really interesting. It's kind of like if you can sell estate planning you can sell anything. You know it has to do with making plans for when you pass away how you leave your inheritance what you say in your will all those kinds of things. The book is is really dry. It's a subject that a lot of people don't want to think about. So I think it'll be really interesting to take a look at how I sold this. This is a direct mail piece. It mailed for many years and the only thing that made them stop mailing was that the law was changed pretty substantially about estate planning and about inheritance and inheritance taxes and so the book just became really out of date. But it mailed for a very long time and it was a challenge to do because it made me really have to think about and figure out how you make something like estate planning not only interesting enough for people to want to read about it and find out about it and buy a book about it but also important enough to get them past their own you know not wanting to confront the idea that one day they'll die. So let's go and take a look. So let's take a little overview first and then we'll do a little bit of a deeper dive. So this is the first page of the letter inside. Now again this is direct mail but don't let that think that it doesn't apply to what you do. It could just as easily be a sales page on the web and in fact it was converted to a sales page on the web. So this is what this is the envelope also by the way. So the outer envelope had this on it as well what every wife wishes her husband knew about estate planning and the IRS hopes you never find out. Really I think it's a really good headline in that it really calls out what this is you know who it's for and it also speaks to the need for it in a in a sort of an indirect fun way. Maybe fun is not exactly the word but in a way that's at least interesting and it's kind of based on a on an old Woody Allen movie something like what every what everyone wants to know about sex but was is afraid to ask or something like that or a book about sex that was that was very well known back in the day. And so there's a little bit of current events there's a little bit of cultural resonance to the headline. We went over the headline what every wife wishes her husband knew about estate planning the IRS hopes you never find out. And then it opens with a story about a state a U.S. Senator Robert Kerr of Oklahoma when he died he left in a state valued at 20 million and it describes how the IRS immediately demanded nine million from his heirs but they would have to sell stocks and real estate at a substantial loss just to pay the sudden estate taxes. They liquidated what they could they had to borrow the other six if they could only raise three million they had to borrow the other six million. He read that right they had to borrow the money in the end most of the estate went to interest payments and the IRS not to the needy and frustrated heirs they went into debt. So it's a very powerful story of what could happen and just in case you're thinking that it can't happen to you says if this can happen to the 20 million dollar fortune of a U.S. Senator and long-standing member of the of the Senate Finance Committee you and I are certainly fair game right so this really brings it home and it also kind of this you and I kind of like we're in this together the reader and the and the writer of this. lane uh with this promotion it's always good to see your copy you know a couple of years later and and and kind of look at it from a distance and and see what you did and again it's direct mail but so all these principles apply to online they apply to to anything that you do we need to really dig deeply to find the benefits of your product and and present them in a compelling and interesting way so thanks and i'll see you later
